title: Phlox woodhousei
intro: Phlox woodhousei, also known as Wood's Phlox, is a perennial herbaceous plant native to the southeastern United States, belonging to the Phlox genus of flowering plants. The plant is valued for its medicinal properties, which include anti-inflammatory, antiseptic, and antimicrobial effects, making it useful in treating various health conditions such as wounds, fever, and respiratory issues. Bioactive constituents of Phlox woodhousei include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and volatile oils, which contribute to its therapeutic properties. The most common herbal preparations made from Phlox woodhousei include infusions, tinctures, and salves, which are often used to treat skin irritations, colds, and coughs.
taxonomy: Phlox woodhousei is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Polemoniaceae. It belongs to the genus Phlox, which consists of about 60 species of flowering plants. Phlox woodhousei is native to the United States and is found in the southwestern region, particularly in the states of Arizona and New Mexico. The classification of Phlox woodhousei is further categorized under the kingdom Plantae, the division Magnoliophyta, and the class Magnoliopsida. This classification helps to understand the plant's evolutionary relationships and its place within the plant kingdom.
